content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Jawaker: Games & Friends",
  "game_name": "Ludo Dash",
  "genre": "Board Game",
  "core_mechanics": "Ludo Dash implements a simplified, single-player version of the classic Ludo board game against an AI opponent. The core mechanics revolve around turn-based gameplay, dice rolling, and strategic piece movement. Player actions are primarily triggered by keyboard input.\n\n**Specific Player Actions and Triggers:**\n*   **Roll Dice:** Triggered by pressing the 'Space' key. This action generates a random integer between 1 and 6 for the current turn, which dictates potential piece movements.\n*   **Select Piece for Movement:** If multiple player pieces are eligible to move based on the current `diceValue`, players use 'ArrowUp' or 'ArrowDown' to cycle through the available, highlighted pieces.\n*   **Confirm Piece Movement:** Once a piece is selected and highlighted, pressing 'Space' again confirms the move, initiating the piece's animation along the board path.\n\n**Exact Rules and Systems Governing Gameplay:**\n*   **Turn-Based System:** The game progresses in turns, alternating between the Player and an AI opponent. The Player always starts first.\n*   **Dice Roll Rules:**\n    *   Rolling a '6' grants the current player an additional turn.\n    *   A piece must roll a '6' to move from its initial 'home base' area onto the main game track's starting square.\n    *   If a player rolls the dice and has no valid moves for any of their pieces, their turn automatically ends.\n*   **Piece Movement Rules:**\n    *   Pieces move clockwise along a predefined path of squares on the board.\n    *   If a player's piece lands on a square occupied by an AI piece (and that square is not a designated 'safe spot'), the AI's piece is 'cut' and immediately returned to its starting home base.\n    *   Designated 'safe spots' (visually marked) protect pieces from being cut.\n    *   To complete the game, a piece must enter its unique 'home column' and then land precisely on the central 'home square' based on the exact dice roll. Any 'over-roll' in the home column means the piece must wait for a future turn with an exact roll to enter the central home square.\n*   **AI Logic:** The AI's decision-making process varies by level difficulty. Generally, it prioritizes bringing pieces out with a '6', then cutting player pieces, and finally moving pieces optimally (e.g., closest to home, or based on strategic advantage).\n\n**State Tracking:**\n*   `game.currentPlayer`: An enumeration (`PLAYER`, `AI`) indicating whose turn it is.\n*   `game.diceValue`: An integer (1-6) representing the last dice roll.\n*   `game.playerPieces`: An array of objects, where each object represents a player's piece and contains properties like `{ id: int, currentPathIndex: int, inHomeBase: boolean, inHomeColumn: boolean, isFinished: boolean }`.\n*   `game.aiPieces`: An array of objects, structurally identical to `game.playerPieces`, for the AI's pieces.\n*   `game.boardPath`: An array of `p5.Vector` objects (or `{x, y}` coordinates) defining the exact center points of each square on the Ludo track.\n*   `game.playerHomeEntryIndex`: The specific index within `game.boardPath` where a player piece enters its dedicated home column.\n*   `game.aiHomeEntryIndex`: The specific index within `game.boardPath` where an AI piece enters its dedicated home column.\n*   `game.playerFinishedCount`: An integer tracking how many of the player's pieces have successfully reached the central home square.\n*   `game.aiFinishedCount`: An integer tracking how many of the AI's pieces have successfully reached the central home square.\n*   `game.currentTurnPhase`: An enumeration (`ROLL_DICE`, `SELECT_PIECE`, `ANIMATE_MOVE`, `TURN_END`) to manage the flow within a single turn.",
  "visual_elements": "**Ludo Board:** The main game area, rendered as a static background. It's a cross-shaped track composed of individual squares, clearly delineated home bases, home columns, and a central home square. Colors (e.g., green for the main board, distinct colors for home bases like red/blue) will distinguish different areas.\n\n**Player Pieces:** Four small, red circular tokens. Each piece will have a subtle white border. When a piece is eligible for selection or movement, it will display a pulsating white glow effect (achieved with `p5.stroke()` with varying alpha or `p5.blendMode()` for a shimmering effect).\n\n**AI Pieces:** Four small, blue circular tokens, also with a subtle white border.\n\n**Dice:** A dynamic graphical representation of a six-sided die. During the 'Roll Dice' phase, it will animate with rapid rotation and slight scaling (`p5.rotate()`, `p5.scale()`) before settling to display the `diceValue` using corresponding pips (dots) on its face. (Matter.js could optionally be used for more realistic physics-based dice rolling, but p5.js animation is sufficient).\n\n**UI Elements (All rendered with `p5.text()` and `p5.rect()` for backgrounds where needed):**\n*   **'Roll Dice' Prompt:** A glowing text overlay, e.g., \"PRESS SPACE TO ROLL\", displayed when it's the player's turn and they need to roll the dice.\n*   **Score Display:** Text formatted as \"SCORE: [CurrentScore]\" persistently in the top-right corner of the screen during gameplay.\n*   **Level Indicator:** Text formatted as \"LEVEL: [CurrentLevel]\" persistently in the top-left corner of the screen during gameplay.\n*   **Turn Indicator:** Prominent text (e.g., \"YOUR TURN\" / \"AI'S TURN\") displayed temporarily during the respective player's turn.\n*   **Game Over / Win Screens:** Full-screen overlays with large, impactful text (e.g., \"GAME OVER!\", \"LEVEL COMPLETE!\"), along with the final score, high score, and prompts for restarting or advancing.\n*   **Level Transition Screen:** A simple, full-screen text overlay stating \"LEVEL [X] STARTING...\" for a brief period between levels.\n\n**Animations and Visual Effects:**\n*   **Dice Roll Animation:** A brief, rapid spinning and resizing animation of the dice graphic before it settles to show the `diceValue`.\n*   **Piece Movement Animation:** Pieces will move smoothly, step-by-step, from the center of their current square to the center of their target square using linear interpolation (`p5.Vector.lerp()`) over a short duration.\n*   **Piece 'Cut' Animation:** When an AI piece is cut, it will quickly shrink, fade out (`p5.alpha()`), and then instantly reappear in its home base.\n*   **Piece Selection Highlight:** Eligible player pieces will display a continuous, subtle pulsating glow or outline.\n*   **Win/Lose Effects:** Upon winning a level, a burst of celebratory confetti particles will animate across the screen. Upon losing, a brief screen darkening or desaturation effect might occur.",
  "control_scheme": "The control scheme is designed for keyboard interaction, specifically utilizing a subset of the provided keys for turn-based Ludo gameplay.\n\n*   **Space:**\n    *   **Roll Dice:** When it is the player's turn and the game is in the `ROLL_DICE_PHASE`, pressing 'Space' triggers the dice roll.\n    *   **Confirm Piece Movement:** If multiple player pieces are eligible for movement (during `SELECT_PIECE_PHASE`), and one is highlighted, pressing 'Space' confirms the selection and initiates the piece's move.\n    *   **Menu/Transition Navigation:** On the `INITIAL_MENU` (to start game), `LEVEL_COMPLETE` (to advance level), and `GAME_OVER` (to restart) screens, 'Space' acts as the primary action/confirmation key.\n*   **ArrowUp:** In the `SELECT_PIECE_PHASE` (when multiple pieces can move), 'ArrowUp' cycles the visual selection highlight to the *previous* eligible player piece in the list.\n*   **ArrowDown:** In the `SELECT_PIECE_PHASE` (when multiple pieces can move), 'ArrowDown' cycles the visual selection highlight to the *next* eligible player piece in the list.\n*   **Shift:** Toggles the `PAUSED` state on and off, allowing players to temporarily halt the game.",
  "game_states": "The game flow is managed by a clear state machine, ensuring distinct phases of play and smooth transitions.\n\n*   **`INITIAL_MENU` State:**\n    *   **Display:** Shows the game title \"Ludo Dash\", a prompt \"Start Game (Press Space)\", and the current \"High Score: [HighScore]\".\n    *   **Transition Trigger:** Player presses 'Space'.\n    *   **Next State:** `LEVEL_INTRO`.\n\n*   **`LEVEL_INTRO` State:**\n    *   **Display:** Presents a large, central message \"Level [CurrentLevel]\" for a short duration.\n    *   **Transition Trigger:** Automatic timeout (e.g., 2 seconds).\n    *   **Next State:** `PLAYING`.\n\n*   **`PLAYING` State:**\n    *   **Display:** The main game board, all active pieces, the dice, current score, level indicator, and turn indicator are visible. Game logic for turns, dice rolls, and piece movements is active.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   **Player Win:** If the player gets all 4 pieces home before the AI, transitions to `LEVEL_COMPLETE`.\n        *   **Player Lose:** If the AI gets all 4 pieces home before the player, transitions to `GAME_OVER`.\n        *   **Pause:** Pressing 'Shift' transitions to `PAUSED`.\n\n*   **`PAUSED` State:**\n    *   **Display:** An overlay stating \"PAUSED\" covers the game screen. Game logic is frozen.\n    *   **Transition Trigger:** Player presses 'Shift' again.\n    *   **Next State:** `PLAYING`.\n\n*   **`LEVEL_COMPLETE` State:**\n    *   **Display:** A celebratory message \"LEVEL [CurrentLevel] COMPLETE!\", shows \"Your Score: [CurrentScore]\", and prompts \"Next Level (Press Space)\".\n    *   **Transition Trigger:** Player presses 'Space'.\n    *   **Next State:** `LEVEL_INTRO` (for the next level, if not the final level) or `GAME_OVER` (if all levels are completed, signifying overall game victory).\n\n*   **`GAME_OVER` State:**\n    *   **Display:** Shows \"GAME OVER!\", \"Final Score: [CurrentScore]\", \"High Score: [HighScore]\", and a \"Restart (Press Space)\" prompt.\n    *   **Transition Trigger:** Player presses 'Space'.\n    *   **Next State:** `INITIAL_MENU` (resets current level to 1, current score to 0).",
  "win_conditions": "*   **Win Condition (Per Level):** The player wins the current level if they successfully move all 4 of their game pieces into the central home square before the AI opponent achieves the same goal.\n*   **Overall Game Win Condition:** The player wins the entire game by successfully completing (winning) all 3 defined levels in sequence.",
  "lose_conditions": "*   **Lose Condition (Per Level):** The player loses the current level if the AI opponent successfully moves all 4 of its game pieces into the central home square before the player does.\n*   **Overall Game Lose Condition:** If the player loses any single level, the entire game ends, and they must restart from Level 1.",
  "scoring_system": "*   **Points Awarded:**\n    *   **+50 points:** For each player piece successfully moved from its home base onto the main starting track square.\n    *   **+100 points:** For each AI piece 'cut' (returned to its home base) by a player piece.\n    *   **+200 points:** For each player piece that successfully reaches the central home square.\n    *   **+500 points:** A bonus awarded for winning a level.\n*   **Point Deductions:**\n    *   **-25 points:** For each player piece 'cut' by the AI. The score cannot drop below zero.\n*   **Bonus Points/Multipliers:** Only the fixed level completion bonus is awarded. There are no other multipliers.\n*   **High Score System:** The game will implement a local high score system. The highest score ever achieved across all play sessions will be stored using `localStorage` in the browser. This high score is checked and updated whenever a game ends (`GAME_OVER` state or overall game completion).\n*   **Score Display:**\n    *   During the `PLAYING` state, the current score will be prominently displayed in the top-right corner of the canvas. Format: \"SCORE: [CurrentScore]\" (e.g., `text('SCORE: ' + currentScore, width - 150, 30);`)\n    *   On the `INITIAL_MENU` and `GAME_OVER` screens, the current high score will be displayed prominently. Format: \"HIGH SCORE: [HighScore]\".\n*   **Score Affects Gameplay:** The score does not directly influence gameplay mechanics. It serves as a metric for player performance, encourages replayability for high score chasing, and contributes to the feeling of progression.",
  "level_system": "The game features 3 progressively challenging levels, each introducing new elements or increased AI difficulty.\n\n*   **Level 1: Novice Neighbor**\n    *   **AI Difficulty:** Basic AI logic. The AI primarily focuses on moving any valid piece. It rarely prioritizes cutting player pieces and has a simple strategy for managing multiple pieces or using a '6'.\n    *   **Board Elements:** Standard Ludo board layout with 6 designated 'safe spots' evenly distributed along each player's and AI's track segment.\n    *   **Starting State:** Both the player and the AI begin with all 4 of their pieces in their respective home bases.\n    *   **Advancement:** Win Level 1 to proceed to Level 2.\n\n*   **Level 2: Crafty Competitor**\n    *   **AI Difficulty:** Intermediate AI. The AI's logic is enhanced to prioritize cutting player pieces if a valid move allows it. It has a better understanding of using a '6' (e.g., bringing out a new piece vs. moving an existing one). When multiple moves are possible, it makes slightly more optimal choices, such as moving the piece closest to reaching its home column.\n    *   **Board Elements:** The board now has fewer 'safe spots' \u2013 only 4 per player/AI track segment, increasing the risk of pieces being cut for both sides.\n    *   **Starting State:** To provide an early challenge, the AI starts with 2 of its pieces already on the main track, positioned 5 steps past its starting square. The player starts normally.\n    *   **Advancement:** Win Level 2 to proceed to Level 3.\n\n*   **Level 3: Master Strategist**\n    *   **AI Difficulty:** Advanced AI. This AI is highly aggressive, always prioritizing cutting player pieces. It makes optimal decisions for using a '6' and employs simple look-ahead logic to block player movement or plan future cuts. It attempts to strategically position its pieces.\n    *   **Board Elements:** The board introduces 2 'trap' squares on the player's side of the track. If a player piece lands on a 'trap' square, that piece becomes 'stuck' for 1 player turn (cannot move on the player's next turn). AI pieces are immune to these traps.\n    *   **Starting State:** The AI starts with 3 of its pieces already on the main track (10 steps past its starting square), and one piece already positioned within its home column, giving it a significant head start.\n    *   **Advancement:** Win Level 3 to win the entire game.\n\n*   **Player Advancement:** Players advance to the next level by successfully winning the current level. If a player loses a level, the game ends, and they must restart from Level 1.\n*   **Transition Display:** When moving between levels (after `LEVEL_COMPLETE` and before the next `PLAYING` state), a full-screen message \"LEVEL [X] STARTING...\" will be displayed for approximately 2 seconds.\n*   **Level Indicator:** A clear indicator, \"LEVEL: [CurrentLevel]\", will be displayed in the top-left corner of the screen throughout the `PLAYING` state (e.g., `text('LEVEL: ' + currentLevel, 50, 30);`).",
  "simplifications": "*   **Single-Player Only:** The original concept's robust social and multiplayer features, including live opponents, voice/text chat, friend lists, and player-created clubs, have been entirely removed. The game is a dedicated single-player experience against an AI.\n*   **Single Game Focus:** Instead of implementing a platform with \"over 50 popular card and board games\", this implementation focuses solely on a single, representative game: Ludo. While the underlying code structure could theoretically be modular, the current scope is for a standalone Ludo game.\n*   **Abstracted Progression System:** The complex progression systems, such as global leaderboards, weekly events, unlockable emotes, and player profile enhancements, are simplified. Progression is limited to local high score tracking and advancing through a fixed number of levels.\n*   **Core Mechanics Simplification:** The game's controls are simplified to keyboard input, and complex context-sensitive controls are reduced to direct actions (roll dice, select piece, confirm move). The Ludo ruleset adheres to a common standard, avoiding highly specific or obscure variations.\n*   **Physics Engine (Matter.js) Optionality:** While p5.js will handle all drawing and animations, Matter.js is considered optional for dice physics (for enhanced realism) but not essential for core piece movement, which relies on deterministic logic and `p5.Vector.lerp()` for smooth animation rather than continuous physics simulation. The core game logic does not depend on a physics engine for piece interactions.",
  "implementation_notes": "1.  **p5.js as Primary Framework:** All rendering, animations, and user interface elements will be implemented using p5.js drawing primitives (rect, circle, line, text) and its event handling system.\n2.  **Game State Management:** A clear state machine approach (e.g., using a `currentGameState` variable with enumerated values) will be central to controlling the game flow and transitions between `INITIAL_MENU`, `LEVEL_INTRO`, `PLAYING`, `PAUSED`, `LEVEL_COMPLETE`, and `GAME_OVER`.\n3.  **Modular Code Structure:** Game entities such as `Piece`, `Dice`, and `Board` should be encapsulated within their own classes or functions to maintain organization and facilitate future expansion or modification. AI logic should also be modular to support varying difficulty levels.\n4.  **Score and Level Display Adherence:** The score and current level *must* be visibly displayed on screen at all times during the `PLAYING` state in the specified positions (Score: top-right, Level: top-left).\n5.  **Level Progression and Transition:** Advancing to the next level is conditional on winning the current level. Clear visual feedback, including a dedicated 'LEVEL INTRO' screen, will be provided during transitions between levels.\n6.  **Persistent High Score:** The high score will be saved locally using `localStorage` to ensure persistence across browser sessions, enhancing replayability.\n7.  **Input Handling:** Keyboard input will be managed using p5.js's `keyPressed()` and `keyReleased()` functions to ensure responsive and context-aware control.\n8.  **Matter.js (Optional):** While p5.js handles most visual and logical aspects, Matter.js could be selectively integrated for more realistic dice rolling physics (e.g., dice bouncing and rotating) to enhance visual fidelity, but it is not a core requirement for piece movement or general game logic."
}
</task>

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>

Ludo Dash

Welcome to Ludo Dash! Race against an AI opponent to get all 4 of your pieces home first. Roll the dice, move strategically, and cut opponent pieces to send them back. You need a 6 to bring pieces out from home base. Land on opponent pieces to cut them (except on safe spots). Navigate through progressively challenging levels with smarter AI and trickier board conditions. Complete all 3 levels to win!

SPACE: Roll dice / Confirm piece selection / Menu actions | ARROW UP: Select previous piece (when multiple pieces can move) | ARROW DOWN: Select next piece (when multiple pieces can move) | ENTER: Start game from start screen | ESC: Pause / Unpause game | R: Restart (return to start screen)
